38	\section{Motivation: the abelian-group-valued relative Picard sheaf}
39	\label{sec:tensorobj_motivation}
40	
41	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
42	\(k\). In Kleiman's framework (\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor},
43	\cref{def:rel_pic_sharp}), the relative Picard functor
44	\[
45	  \Pic_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \AddCommGrpCat,
46	  \qquad
47	  T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^* \Pic(T),
48	\]
49	is, by definition, abelian-group-valued. The abelian-group structure on each
50	fiber \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) comes from tensor product of line bundles
51	(Stacks tag 01CR / Hartshorne~II.\S 6), and the structure descends to the
52	quotient because \(\pi_T^*\) is a group homomorphism.
53	
54	% SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
55	% Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
56	% abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
57	% groups)
58	% (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
59	% SOURCE QUOTE:
60	% "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
61	%  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
62	%  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
63	% abelian groups defined by the formula
64	% 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
65	% \end{dfn}
66	%  [\dots]
67	% \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
68	%   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
69	% 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
70	%  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
71	% topologies  by
72	% 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
73	% 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
74	%  \end{dfn}"
75	\textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2, Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs.
76	The absolute and relative Picard functors take values in the category of
77	abelian groups; the relative functor is the quotient of one such group by
78	another, and the structure morphisms of the resulting presheaf are group
79	homomorphisms.}
80	
81	Concretely, the abelian-group law on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is the assignment
82	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr] :=
83	\bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
84	with neutral element the class of the structure sheaf
85	\(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) and inverse
86	\(- \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\), where
87	\(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}}(\mathcal{L},
88	\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the dual invertible sheaf. Pullback of
89	\(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) preserves tensor
90	products and the structure sheaf, so the image
91	\(\pi_T^* \Pic(T) \subset \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a subgroup, and the quotient
92	\(\Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\) inherits a canonical abelian-group
93	structure. This is the content of
94	\cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid} in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor}.
95	
96	The mathematics is straightforward; the obstacle to formalisation is purely
97	infrastructural. The Lean construction of the abelian-group law
98	\[
99	  \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
100	  \;:=\; \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
101	\]
102	on isomorphism classes of line bundles requires three ingredients on the
103	Lean carrier:
104	\begin{enumerate}
105	  \item a binary tensor-product operation
106	    \(\otimes\, : \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
107	                  \to \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\);
108	  \item the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X\) as a designated unit object
109	    for \(\otimes\);
110	  \item an inverse operation on the full subcategory of invertible objects,
111	    i.e.\ the dual \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L},
112	    \mathcal{O}_X)\) of an invertible sheaf.
113	\end{enumerate}
114	At Mathlib's pinned commit, only a presheaf-level version of (1) is
115	available; (2) and (3) are present as scheme-level objects, but the
116	binary operation in (1) that ties them together at the
117	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) level is missing. This chapter records the
118	project-side substrate that supplies (1) and consequently lifts (2) +
119	(3) into a monoidal-category structure on \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\).
120	
121	\section{Mathlib API survey}
122	\label{sec:tensorobj_api_survey}
123	
124	The relevant pieces of the Mathlib API at the pinned commit
125	(\texttt{b80f227}) split across two layers.
126	
127	\paragraph{Layer 1: presheaf-of-modules monoidal structure.} Mathlib's
128	\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules} module
129	constructs a monoidal-category structure on the category
130	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\,R\) of presheaves of modules over a presheaf of
131	commutative rings \(R\); in particular, it exposes a tensor-product
132	operation \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) realising the
133	sectionwise tensor product
134	\[
135	  (M \otimes_R N)(U) \;\;=\;\; M(U) \otimes_{R(U)} N(U)
136	\]
137	for open sets / objects \(U\) in the indexing site, together with the
138	associator, left/right unitors, and braiding making the resulting
139	category symmetric monoidal. This is the presheaf-level analogue of
140	Stacks tag 03DM (relative tensor product of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules).
141	
142	\paragraph{Layer 2: scheme-level \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules.} The module
143	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) on a scheme \(X\) (the Lean target
144	expressed by the namespace \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules})
145	realises the category of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules
146	on the small Zariski site of \(X\). It comes with pullback functoriality
147	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullback}\), used heavily in
148	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}), pullback comparison isomorphisms
149	(\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp}\),
150	\(\mathtt{Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr}\)), and the structure-sheaf
151	constant module \(\mathcal{O}_X\). It does \emph{not} ship a binary
152	tensor-product operation
153	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \times \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \to
154	\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), nor a \(\MonoidalCategory\) instance.
155	
156	\paragraph{The gap.} The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)
157	this chapter introduces is the named operation lifting
158	\(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) from Layer 1 into
159	Layer 2, packaged so that the consumer at chapter
160	\cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} can read it as a binary operation on
161	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) together with associator,
162	unitor, and braiding data. The project's policy on this gap is
163	\emph{project-side}: the substrate is built inside
164	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/} (a fresh file, sketched
165	\(\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/TensorObjSubstrate.lean}\), is the
166	natural home) and the consumer instance is closed against it. A
167	parallel Mathlib upstream PR with the same content is welcome but not
168	on the critical path for the project; the consumer is closed against
169	the project-side substrate independently of any upstream timing.
170	
171	\section{The substrate \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)}
172	\label{sec:tensorobj_substrate}
173	
174	\begin{definition}
175	
176	\leanok
177	[The substrate operation $\otimes$ on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
178	  \label{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
179	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj}
180	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. For
181	  \(M, N \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), define
182	  \[
183	    M \otimes_X N \;\;\in\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
184	  \]
185	  as follows. Choose an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}_{i \in I}\)
186	  of \(X\). On each \(U_i\), the restriction
187	  \(M|_{U_i}\) corresponds (via the equivalence between
188	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\Spec A_i}\)-modules and \(A_i\)-modules) to an
189	  \(A_i\)-module \(M_i\), and similarly
190	  \(N|_{U_i}\) corresponds to an \(A_i\)-module \(N_i\). Define
191	  \((M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\) as the sheafification of the presheaf
192	  \(U_i \mapsto M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i\). On overlaps
193	  \(U_i \cap U_j = \Spec A_{ij}\) the resulting modules agree via the
194	  canonical compatibility
195	  \((M_i \otimes_{A_i} N_i) \otimes_{A_i} A_{ij}
196	     \;\cong\;
197	     (M_j \otimes_{A_j} N_j) \otimes_{A_j} A_{ij}\),
198	  so the data \(\{(M \otimes_X N)|_{U_i}\}\) glue to a single object
199	  \(M \otimes_X N\) in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). The result is
200	  canonically independent of the affine cover chosen.
201	
202	  Equivalently: the underlying presheaf of \(M \otimes_X N\) is the
203	  presheaf-of-modules tensor product
204	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) of the underlying
205	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\), composed with the sheafification
206	  functor on the small Zariski site of \(X\). The substrate operation
207	  \(\otimes_X\) is, by construction, a \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
208	  object reflecting the relative tensor product of
209	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described in Stacks tag 03DM.
210	\end{definition}
211	
212	\begin{lemma}
213	
214	\leanok
215	[Functoriality of $\otimes_X$]
216	  \label{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
217	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.tensorObj_functoriality}
218	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
219	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The assignment
220	  \((M, N) \mapsto M \otimes_X N\) of
221	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} extends to a bifunctor
222	  \[
223	    \otimes_X \;\;\colon\;\; \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\times\;
224	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X \;\;\longrightarrow\;\;
225	                          \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X
226	  \]
227	  natural in both arguments: a pair of morphisms
228	  \(f : M \to M'\) and \(g : N \to N'\) determines a morphism
229	  \(f \otimes g : M \otimes_X N \to M' \otimes_X N'\) compatible with
230	  identities and composition. The functor preserves the structure
231	  sheaf as a unit, in the sense that there are canonical natural
232	  isomorphisms
233	  \[
234	    \lambda_M \;\colon\; \mathcal{O}_X \otimes_X M \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
235	    \qquad
236	    \rho_M \;\colon\; M \otimes_X \mathcal{O}_X \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\; M,
237	  \]
238	  and a canonical associator natural isomorphism
239	  \[
240	    \alpha_{M, N, P} \;\colon\;
241	    (M \otimes_X N) \otimes_X P
242	      \;\xrightarrow{\sim}\;
243	    M \otimes_X (N \otimes_X P)
244	  \]
245	  and a canonical braiding
246	  \(\beta_{M, N} : M \otimes_X N \xrightarrow{\sim} N \otimes_X M\), each
247	  inherited from the corresponding data on
248	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\) via the sheafification functor
249	  of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}.
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
254	  \leanok
255	  Pick an affine open cover \(\{U_i = \Spec A_i\}\) of \(X\). On each
256	  affine, the assignment is the standard tensor product of modules over a
257	  commutative ring \(A_i\), which is bifunctorial, has the ring as a
258	  monoidal unit, and is associative and symmetric up to canonical
259	  natural isomorphism. These data glue across the cover because the
260	  underlying gluing data of \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} respect
261	  the canonical morphisms on overlaps. Equivalently, all four pieces of
262	  natural-isomorphism data (\(\lambda, \rho, \alpha, \beta\)) are
263	  inherited verbatim from the corresponding data on
264	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal.tensorObj}\) (Mathlib module
265	  \texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.PresheafOfModules}) under
266	  sheafification; the sheafification functor on the small Zariski site
267	  is symmetric monoidal because it preserves the relevant filtered
268	  colimits and finite limits used to construct the monoidal structure
269	  on \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules}\).
270	\end{proof}
271	
272	\begin{theorem}
273	
274	\leanok
275	[Monoidal-category structure on $\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X$]
276	  \label{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}
277	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory}
278	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
279	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. The bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) of
280	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, with unit object
281	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\), associator \(\alpha\), left unitor \(\lambda\),
282	  right unitor \(\rho\), and braiding \(\beta\) of
283	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}, satisfies the
284	  pentagon, triangle, and hexagon axioms of a symmetric monoidal
285	  category. Consequently \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) carries a
286	  canonical symmetric \(\MonoidalCategory\) structure.
287	\end{theorem}
288	
289	\begin{proof}
290	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality}
291	  \leanok
292	  % NOTE (review iter-203): the proof-block \leanok above is INCORRECT. The Lean
293	  % instance AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.monoidalCategory has a `:= sorry`
294	  % body (intentional contamination guard, iter-203 — confirmed sorryAx by the
295	  % lean-auditor). A proof-block \leanok asserts "proof closed, no sorry", so it
296	  % launders this declaration. sync_leanok (iter-203) did not remove it. \leanok
297	  % is sync-owned and the review agent may not delete it; next plan/sync pass
298	  % must strip this stray proof-block \leanok. The statement-block \leanok (L274)
299	  % is correct (a sorry-bodied decl exists).
300	  The pentagon and triangle identities (associator and unitor coherence)
301	  are inherited from the analogous identities on
302	  \(\mathtt{PresheafOfModules.Monoidal}\). On any affine open
303	  \(\Spec A \subset X\), the assertions reduce to the analogous
304	  identities for the tensor product of \(A\)-modules, which are
305	  standard. The gluing data on overlaps is preserved by all four
306	  natural isomorphisms by construction, so the affine-level coherence
307	  identities yield global coherence identities on
308	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\). Symmetry of \(\beta\) follows by the
309	  same affine descent argument.
310	\end{proof}
311	
312	\section{The lift through \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)}
313	\label{sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
314	
315	The consumer in chapter \cref{chap:Picard_RelPicFunctor} works not with
316	\(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_k T)\) directly but with the subtype
317	\(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) of locally-trivial
318	modules on the fiber product \(C \times_k T\), introduced in
319	\cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}. This section spells out how the
320	monoidal-category structure of
321	\cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to that subtype, and how
322	the restriction interacts with the pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\).
323	
324	\begin{lemma}
325	[Tensor product of locally-trivial modules is locally trivial]
326	  \label{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
327	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj}
328	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, and let
329	  \(L, L' \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\) be locally-trivial modules of
330	  rank one (i.e.\ line bundles). The tensor product \(L \otimes_X L'\) of
331	  \cref{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj} is again locally trivial of rank
332	  one. Consequently the bifunctor \(\otimes_X\) restricts to a bifunctor
333	  on the full subcategory \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X \subset \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\)
334	  of locally-trivial rank-one modules.
335	\end{lemma}
336	
337	\begin{proof}
338	  Pick a common affine open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) on which both
339	  \(L|_{U_i}\) and \(L'|_{U_i}\) are free of rank one. On each \(U_i\)
340	  there are trivialisations \(L|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\) and
341	  \(L'|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). The tensor product of two free
342	  rank-one modules over a commutative ring is again free of rank one;
343	  hence \((L \otimes_X L')|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}\). So
344	  \(L \otimes_X L'\) is locally trivial of rank one, i.e.\ an object of
345	  \(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X\).
346	\end{proof}
347	
348	\begin{lemma}
349	[Inverse of an invertible module]
350	  \label{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
351	  \uses{def:scheme_modules_tensorobj, lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
352	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(L \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,X\) be a line
353	  bundle. The dual
354	  \[
355	    L^{-1} \;\;:=\;\; \mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(L, \mathcal{O}_X)
356	  \]
357	  is again a line bundle on \(X\), and there is a canonical natural
358	  isomorphism
359	  \(\varepsilon_L : L \otimes_X L^{-1} \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_X\).
360	  Consequently every line bundle has a two-sided tensor inverse under
361	  \(\otimes_X\), and the monoid \(\bigl(\texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim, \otimes_X,
362	  \mathcal{O}_X\bigr)\) of isomorphism classes is an abelian group ---
363	  the Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) (Stacks tag 01CR).
364	\end{lemma}
365	
366	\begin{proof}
367	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial}
368	  Locally trivial of rank one is preserved by
369	  \(\mathcal{H}om(-, \mathcal{O}_X)\): on a common affine trivialising
370	  cover, the dual of a free rank-one module is again free of rank one.
371	  The contraction map
372	  \(L \otimes_X \mathcal{H}om(L, \mathcal{O}_X) \to \mathcal{O}_X\) is a
373	  morphism in \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\,X\), and on each open of the
374	  trivialising cover it specialises to the contraction
375	  \(A \otimes_A A \to A\) of free rank-one \(A\)-modules, which is an
376	  isomorphism; hence the contraction is a global isomorphism. The
377	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(X) := \texttt{LineBundle}\,X / \sim\)
378	  is then standard (Stacks tag 01CR).
379	\end{proof}
380	
381	\begin{lemma}
382	[Restriction to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\)]
383	  \label{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
384	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
385	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
386	  Let \(C \to S\) and \(T \to S\) be \(S\)-schemes and consider the
387	  \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct} carrier on \(C \times_S T\) of
388	  \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, concretised as the subtype
389	  \[
390	    \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
391	    \;\;=\;\;
392	    \bigl\{\,M \in \Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}(C \times_S T)
393	            \,\bigm|\, \mathtt{IsLocallyTrivial}\,M \,\bigr\}.
394	  \]
395	  The bifunctor \(\otimes_{C \times_S T}\) of
396	  \cref{lem:scheme_modules_tensorobj_functoriality} restricts to a
397	  bifunctor on \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) with unit
398	  the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\), and the dual
399	  \(L \mapsto L^{-1}\) of \cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} is a
400	  two-sided tensor inverse on this subcategory.
401	\end{lemma}
402	
403	\begin{proof}
404	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
405	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}
406	  Direct from
407	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_preserves_locally_trivial,
408	        lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible} applied to
409	  \(X := C \times_S T\): the tensor product of two locally-trivial
410	  rank-one modules is locally trivial of rank one, and the dual is
411	  again locally trivial of rank one. The unit \(\mathcal{O}_{C
412	  \times_S T}\) is locally trivial of rank one (trivially), so it
413	  belongs to \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\). The
414	  monoidal-category structure of
415	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricts to the subcategory by
416	  the same affine descent argument.
417	\end{proof}
418	
419	\begin{lemma}
420	[\(\pi_T^*\) is a tensor-functor]
421	  \label{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}
422	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
423	  With notation as in \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}, the pullback
424	  functor
425	  \[
426	    \pi_T^{*} \;\;\colon\;\; \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \;\longrightarrow\;
427	                          \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T
428	  \]
429	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a (strong) monoidal
430	  functor: there is a canonical natural isomorphism
431	  \(\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_T \mathcal{N'})
432	     \xrightarrow{\sim}
433	   \pi_T^* \mathcal{N} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \pi_T^* \mathcal{N'}\)
434	  for \(\mathcal{N}, \mathcal{N'} \in \texttt{LineBundle}\,T\), and a
435	  canonical isomorphism
436	  \(\pi_T^* \mathcal{O}_T \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{O}_{C \times_S T}\),
437	  both inherited from the analogous data on the underlying
438	  \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}\) functors via the
439	  pullback-tensor-compatibility of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules.
440	\end{lemma}
441	
442	\begin{proof}
443	  \uses{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}
444	  On any affine open of \(T\) over which \(\mathcal{N}\) and
445	  \(\mathcal{N'}\) trivialise simultaneously, the assertion reduces to
446	  the standard fact that the extension of scalars
447	  \(B \otimes_A (M \otimes_A M')
448	   \;\cong\; (B \otimes_A M) \otimes_B (B \otimes_A M')\)
449	  for a ring map \(A \to B\) and \(A\)-modules \(M, M'\). This affine
450	  identity glues across an affine trivialising cover of \(T\) by the
451	  same descent argument as in
452	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal}. The compatibility with the
453	  unit, the associator, and the unitors is verified pointwise on the
454	  same cover.
455	\end{proof}
456	
457	\section{Consumer: the \(\AddCommGroup\) instance for the relative Picard sheaf}
458	\label{sec:tensorobj_consumer_acg}
459	
460	The substrate of \cref{sec:tensorobj_substrate,sec:tensorobj_onproduct_lift}
461	supplies all the data missing from \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}'s Lean
462	encoding. The consumer instance below packages the abelian-group law
463	\(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
464	 := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_S T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\) on the
465	relative Picard quotient via the substrate.
466	
467	\begin{theorem}
468	
469	\leanok
470	[Abelian-group instance on the relative Picard quotient via \(\Scheme.\mathtt{Modules}.\mathtt{tensorObj}\)]
471	  \label{thm:rel_pic_addcommgroup_via_tensorobj}
472	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup_via_tensorObj}
473	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
474	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
475	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
476	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
477	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
478	
479	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
480	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard functors are
481	  % abelian-group-valued; the relative one is defined as a quotient of abelian
482	  % groups)
483	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
484	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
485	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:aPf}
486	  %  The {\it absolute Picard functor} $\Pic_X$ is the functor from  the
487	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) $S$-schemes $T$ to the category of
488	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
489	  % 	$$\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).$$
490	  % \end{dfn}
491	  %  [\dots]
492	  % \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
493	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} $\Pic_{X/S}$ is defined by
494	  % 	$$\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).$$
495	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
496	  % topologies  by
497	  % 	$$\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
498	  % 	\Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.$$
499	  %  \end{dfn}"
500	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
501	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
502	  groups; the relative Picard functor is a quotient of abelian groups).}
503	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve over a
504	  field \(k\), let \(\pi_C : C \to \Spec k\) be the structure morphism,
505	  and let \(T\) be a \(k\)-scheme with structure morphism
506	  \(\pi_T : T \to \Spec k\). The set
507	  \[
508	    \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
509	  \]
510	  of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries a canonical
511	  abelian-group structure with addition
512	  \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] + \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]
513	   := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L} \otimes_{C \times_k T} \mathcal{L}'\,\bigr]\),
514	  neutral element \(\bigl[\,\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\,\bigr]\), and
515	  inverse
516	  \(-\bigl[\,\mathcal{L}\,\bigr] := \bigl[\,\mathcal{L}^{-1}\,\bigr]\),
517	  where \(\otimes_{C \times_k T}\) and \((-)^{-1}\) are the operations
518	  supplied by the substrate of
519	  \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} restricted to the
520	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) carrier via
521	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct}. With this structure the quotient
522	  map
523	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is a
524	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\),
525	  in agreement with \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
526	\end{theorem}
527	
528	\begin{proof}
529	  \uses{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal,
530	        lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,
531	        lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj,
532	        thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined,
533	        lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
534	  By
535	  \cref{lem:tensorobj_lift_onproduct,lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible},
536	  the substrate restricts to an abelian-group structure on the set
537	  \(\texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T / \sim\) of isomorphism
538	  classes of line bundles on \(C \times_k T\). Concretely:
539	  associativity and commutativity come from the monoidal-category
540	  axioms of \cref{thm:scheme_modules_monoidal} (pentagon, triangle,
541	  hexagon coherence on isomorphism classes); the neutral element is
542	  the class of \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (left/right unitor
543	  coherence); and the inverse is the class of
544	  \(\mathcal{L}^{-1} = \mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C
545	  \times_k T})\) (\cref{lem:tensorobj_inverse_invertible}).
546	
547	  By \cref{lem:pullback_compatible_with_tensorobj}, the pullback
548	  functor
549	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \texttt{LineBundle}\,T \to
550	   \texttt{LineBundle.OnProduct}\,\pi_C\,\pi_T\) is a strong monoidal
551	  functor; in particular it carries products to products and preserves
552	  the unit, so the induced map on isomorphism classes
553	  \(\pi_T^{*} : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) is a group
554	  homomorphism. Its image is therefore a subgroup of
555	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\).
556	
557	  By \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}, the underlying set
558	  of \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) is the set of right cosets of this
559	  subgroup. The quotient by a subgroup of an abelian group is itself
560	  an abelian group, and the quotient map is a surjective group
561	  homomorphism whose kernel is exactly the subgroup --- the standard
562	  fact (Stacks tag 01CR for the Picard-group special case; the
563	  general quotient-of-abelian-groups statement is in any algebra
564	  textbook). Putting the pieces together gives the
565	  \(\AddCommGroup\) instance on \(\Pic^{\sharp}_{C/k}(T)\) of the
566	  statement, and identifies it with the structure of
567	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}.
568	\end{proof}

14	\section{Setup and motivation}
15	\label{sec:relspec_setup}
16	
17	The Route~A construction of \(J = \Pic^0_{C/k}\) as a \(g\)-dimensional abelian variety is
18	organised around the relative Picard functor
19	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T) / \pi_T^* \Pic(T)\)
20	on the category of \(k\)-schemes. Representing \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) requires the line-bundle
21	pullback construction on a relative curve \(C \times_k T \to T\), which in turn rests on a
22	\emph{relative scheme} construction on a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras
23	\(\mathcal{A}\), the \emph{relative spectrum}
24	\(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\). This chapter packages the construction
25	of \(\underline{\Spec}_X(-)\) and its three core properties --- universal property, affine
26	base, base change --- as the foundational A.1.a building block. The relative Picard
27	functor itself (A.1.c), the line-bundle pullback functor on a product (A.1.b), and the
28	identity-component / dimension theory of \(J\) (A.3) live in separate sibling chapters
29	(\S~``Out of scope'' below).
30	
31	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section ``Relative spectrum via glueing'' and ``Relative
32	% spectrum as a functor'' (tags 01LL, 01LO, 01LQ, 01LR, 01LS, available locally at
33	% references/stacks-constructions.tex L309-L720). The Hartshorne reference is
34	% [Hartshorne], II Exercise 5.17 (read from references/hartshorne-algebraic-geometry.pdf).
35	
36	\section{Quasi-coherent sheaves of algebras}
37	\label{sec:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
38	
39	\begin{definition}\leanok
40	[Quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra]
41	  \label{def:qc_sheaf_of_algebras}
42	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QcohAlgebra}
43	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Section 01LL ``Relative spectrum via glueing'',
44	  % Situation ``situation-relative-spec''
45	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L312-L318).
46	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
47	  % "Here \(S\) is a scheme, and \(\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent
48	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebra. This means that \(\mathcal{A}\) is a
49	  %  sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-algebras which is quasi-coherent as an
50	  %  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module."
51	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LL (situation-relative-spec).}
52	  Let \(X\) be a scheme. A \emph{quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras} is a
53	  sheaf \(\mathcal{A}\) of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras (i.e. an \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
54	  equipped with \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-linear multiplication and unit morphisms satisfying
55	  associativity, commutativity, and the unit law as sheaf morphisms) whose underlying
56	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module is quasi-coherent. We write \(\mathrm{QcohAlg}(X)\) for the
57	  category of quasi-coherent sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebras and
58	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra morphisms.
59	\end{definition}
60

79	\section{The relative-spectrum scheme}
80	\label{sec:relspec_scheme}
81	
82	The construction is given affine-locally: on every affine open \(U = \Spec R \subseteq X\) one
83	sets \(\pi^{-1}(U) := \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) regarded as an \(R\)-algebra, and the local pieces
84	glue compatibly because \(\mathcal{A}\) is quasi-coherent (the transition isomorphism
85	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for an inclusion
86	\(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\) gives an open immersion of the corresponding Specs).
87	
88	\begin{theorem}\leanok
89	[Relative spectrum exists]
90	  \label{thm:relative_spec_exists}
91	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec}
92	  
93	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec)
94	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L381-L405).
95	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
96	  % "In Situation REF.
97	  %  There exists a morphism of schemes
98	  %  \(\)
99	  %  \pi : \underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow S
100	  %  \(\)
101	  %  with the following properties:
102	  %  \begin{enumerate}
103	  %  \item for every affine open \(U \subset S\) there exists an isomorphism
104	  %  \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \to \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\), and
105	  %  \item for \(U \subset U' \subset S\) affine open the composition
106	  %  \(\)
107	  %  \xymatrix{
108	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \ar[r]^{i_U^{-1}} &
109	  %  \pi^{-1}(U) \ar[rr]^{inclusion} & &
110	  %  \pi^{-1}(U') \ar[r]^{i_{U'}} &
111	  %  \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))
112	  %  }
113	  %  \(\)
114	  %  is the open immersion of Lemma REF above.
115	  %  \end{enumerate}
116	  %  Moreover, \(\underline{\Spec}_S(\mathcal{A})\)
117	  %  is unique up to unique isomorphism over \(S\)."
118	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ (lemma-glue-relative-spec);
119	  cf.\ [Hartshorne], II~Ex.~5.17(a).}
120	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\mathcal{A}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-algebra
121	  (Definition~REF). There exists a scheme
122	  \(\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})\) together with an affine morphism
123	  \(\pi : \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}) \to X\) such that
124	  \begin{enumerate}
125	    \item for every affine open \(U \subseteq X\), there is an isomorphism
126	      \(i_U : \pi^{-1}(U) \xrightarrow{\sim} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) over \(U\);
127	    \item for \(U \subseteq U' \subseteq X\) both affine open, the composite
128	      $\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \xrightarrow{i_U^{-1}} \pi^{-1}(U) \hookrightarrow
129	      \pi^{-1}(U') \xrightarrow{i_{U'}} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))$
130	      coincides with the open immersion induced by the restriction
131	      \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\).
132	  \end{enumerate}
133	  The pair \((\underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A}), \pi)\) is unique up to unique isomorphism
134	  over \(X\).
135	\end{theorem}
136	
137	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Follows immediately from
138	% Lemmas REF,
139	% REF, and
140	% REF.
141	% Uniqueness is stated in the last sentence of
142	% Lemma REF."
143	\begin{proof}
144	  
145	  
146	\leanok
147	  By the open-cover gluing principle for schemes (Mathlib
148	  \texttt{Scheme.GlueData} / \texttt{AffineScheme.glueOpens}) it suffices to specify
149	  affine local pieces \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U))\) for each affine open \(U \subseteq X\) and
150	  open-immersion transition data
151	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) \hookrightarrow \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\) for \(U \subseteq U'\)
152	  satisfying the cocycle condition. The transition morphisms come from the ring maps
153	  \(\mathcal{A}(U') \to \mathcal{A}(U)\); quasi-coherence yields the cartesian square
154	  \(\Spec(\mathcal{A}(U)) = U \times_{U'} \Spec(\mathcal{A}(U'))\), so each transition is an
155	  open immersion. Transitivity (Stacks lemma-transitive-spec) gives the cocycle condition.
156	  Uniqueness of the glued scheme is the standard property of the gluing functor.
157	\end{proof}
158	
159	\begin{theorem}\leanok
160	[Universal property of the relative spectrum]
161	  \label{thm:relative_spec_univ}
162	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelativeSpec.UniversalProperty}
163	  
164	  % NOTE (iter-173 review): the iter-173 file-skeleton encodes this as the
165	  % structural consequence ``IsAffineHom (structureMorphism 𝒜)'' rather than

168	  % witness (or equivalent) before any consumer file relies on this theorem.
169	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], tag 01LQ continuation: Section ``Relative spectrum as
170	  % a functor'', functor F and Lemma lemma-spec
171	  % (read from references/stacks-constructions.tex, L427-L466 + L547-L551).
172	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
173	  % "For any \(f : T \to S\) the pullback
174	  %  \(f^*\mathcal{A}\) is a quasi-coherent sheaf of \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-algebras.
175	  %  We are going to consider pairs \((f : T \to S, \varphi)\) where
176	  %  \(f\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\varphi : f^*\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{O}_T\)

494	\section{Lean encoding}
495	\label{sec:relspec_lean_encoding}
496	
497	The Lean target file is the new \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}.
498	The construction proceeds by gluing absolute affine pieces: for each affine open
499	\(U \subseteq X\) with \(U = \Spec R\) and \(A := \mathcal{A}(U)\) regarded as an
500	\(R\)-algebra, the local piece is \(\Spec A\) with structure morphism \(\Spec A \to \Spec R = U\)
501	induced by the algebra unit \(R \to A\). Mathlib provides \texttt{IsAffineOpen.toScheme} for
502	the affine-open inclusion and the absolute \texttt{Scheme.Spec} functor for the local
503	pieces; the transition isomorphisms come from quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{A}\) via
504	\texttt{QuasiCoherent.tensorIso} (or the explicit
505	\(\mathcal{A}(U) \otimes_R S \xrightarrow{\sim} \mathcal{A}(V)\) for \(V = \Spec S \subseteq U\)).
506	The gluing backbone is \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.GlueData} (general construction) or
507	the convenience wrapper \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.AffineScheme.glueOpens}; the cocycle
508	condition reduces to Stacks \texttt{lemma-transitive-spec}. The universal property
509	(Theorem~REF) is expressed as a
510	\texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} witness, providing the natural bijection
511	$\Hom_X(T, \underline{\Spec}_X(\mathcal{A})) \cong
512	\Hom_{\mathcal{O}_X\text{-alg}}(\mathcal{A}, g_*\mathcal{O}_T)$ as a
513	\texttt{Yoneda}-style structure. The base-change isomorphism
514	(Theorem~REF) and functoriality
515	(Theorem~REF) are derived from the universal property by
516	Yoneda. No new core axiom is required; the entire chapter is a definitional/structural
517	construction on top of existing Mathlib affine-scheme infrastructure.
518

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:lbp_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically
33	integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) (the curve carrying the Jacobian; the chapter's
34	constructions are functorial in \(C\) and do not formally use these hypotheses
35	until they meet the representability theorems in the downstream A.1.c / A.2.*
36	chapters). A test object is a \(k\)-scheme \(T\); the relative curve over \(T\) is
37	\(C \times_k T \xrightarrow{\pi_T} T\), with \(\pi_C : C \times_k T \to C\) the
38	companion projection. Both \(\pi_T\) and \(\pi_C\) are flat (because base change of
39	flat morphisms is flat and \(C/k\) is flat since \(k\) is a field).
40	
41	The relative Picard presheaf compares two natural line-bundle groups on the
42	product: the Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the total space, and the
43	Picard group \(\Pic(T)\) of the base. The pullback functor \(\pi_T^*\) sends a line
44	bundle on \(T\) to its base-change line bundle on \(C \times_k T\); the
45	\emph{relative Picard presheaf}
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)\, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	is the quotient set (and, with extra structure, the quotient abelian group). It
50	is the universal Set-valued functor on \((\Sch/k)^{op}\) that ``forgets line
51	bundles pulled back from the base'', and is the starting point of the
52	representability theorems of Grothendieck, Mumford, and Kleiman (see
53	\cref{chap:Jacobian} for the wider Route~A picture).
54	
55	\section{Line bundles on a relative curve}
56	\label{sec:lbp_line_bundle}
57	
58	\begin{definition}\leanok
59	[Line bundle on a product]
60	  \label{def:line_bundle_on_product}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
62	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
63	  % Definition df:aPf (absolute Picard functor)
64	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
65	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
66	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
67	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
68	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
69	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
70	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
71	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor''
72	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HK, Hartshorne II~§6).}
73	  Let \(k\) be a base field, \(C\) a \(k\)-scheme (in our application, a smooth proper
74	  geometrically integral curve), and \(T\) a \(k\)-scheme. A
75	  \emph{line bundle on the relative curve \(C \times_k T\)} is an invertible
76	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module, that is, an
77	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module \(\mathcal{L}\) such that there exists an
78	  open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(C \times_k T\) on which each restriction
79	  \(\mathcal{L}|_{U_i}\) is free of rank one. Two line bundles are isomorphic if
80	  they are isomorphic as \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-modules. The set of
81	  isomorphism classes forms an abelian group under tensor product (the
82	  \emph{Picard group}), denoted
83	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\). In Kleiman's notation with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\),
84	  this is exactly the value
85	  \(\Pic_C(T) = \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of the absolute Picard functor.
86	\end{definition}

127	\section{Project-side IsLocallyTrivial substrate}
128	\label{sec:lbp_isLocallyTrivial_substrate}
129	
130	Mathlib at the pinned commit \texttt{b80f227} ships no
131	\texttt{Scheme.Modules.IsInvertible} predicate (iter-187 \texttt{lean\_local\_search}
132	verdict: empty). The project introduces a stand-in predicate
133	\texttt{Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial} encoding rank-one local
134	trivialisation on an affine cover. The subtype
135	\texttt{\{ M : (Limits.pullback πC πT).Modules // IsLocallyTrivial M \}}
136	gives the carrier of \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}.
137	
138	\begin{definition}
139	
140	\leanok
141	[Local triviality of a \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module]
142	  \label{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
143	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial}
144	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
145	  (locally trivial $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules), specialised to rank one.}
146	  An \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\) on a scheme \(X\) is
147	  \emph{locally trivial of rank one} if for every point \(x \in X\) there
148	  exists an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an iso
149	  \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\) (the structure sheaf restricted to
150	  \(U\) viewed as the unit object of \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\)). Formally:
151	  \[
152	    \forall\, x : X, \exists\, U : X.\mathrm{Opens},
153	      x \in U \,\wedge\, \mathtt{IsAffineOpen}\, U \,\wedge\,
154	      \mathtt{Nonempty}\, (\mathcal{M}.\mathrm{restrict}\, U.\iota \cong
155	        \mathtt{SheafOfModules.unit}\, \_).
156	  \]
157	\end{definition}
158	
159	\begin{lemma}
160	
161	\leanok
162	[Pullback preserves local triviality (Stacks 01HH)]
163	  \label{lem:IsLocallyTrivial_pullback}
164	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.IsLocallyTrivial.pullback}
165	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
166	  \textit{Source: Stacks~\href{https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/01HH}{01HH}
167	  (pullback of a locally trivial module is locally trivial).}
168	  Let \(f : Y \to X\) be a morphism of schemes and let
169	  \(\mathcal{M} : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) be locally trivial of rank one
170	  (\cref{def:IsLocallyTrivial}). Then \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is locally
171	  trivial of rank one.
172	\end{lemma}
173	\begin{proof}
174	  \uses{def:IsLocallyTrivial}
175	  \leanok
176	  Given \(y : Y\), let \(x = f(y) \in X\). By the local triviality of
177	  \(\mathcal{M}\) at \(x\), there is an affine open \(U \ni x\) and an
178	  iso \(\mathcal{M}|_U \cong \mathcal{O}_U\). The preimage \(f^{-1}U\)
179	  is an open neighbourhood of \(y\), and any sufficiently small affine
180	  open \(V \subseteq f^{-1}U\) containing \(y\) (exists by
181	  \texttt{exists\_isAffineOpen\_mem\_and\_subset}) gives the chart. The
182	  required iso
183	  \((f^*\mathcal{M})|_V \cong \mathcal{O}_V\) follows from the chain:
184	  \texttt{restrictFunctorIsoPullback} (identifying \(\mathrm{restrict}\, V.\iota\)
185	  with \(\mathrm{pullback}\, V.\iota\)), \texttt{pullbackComp} (composing
186	  pullback functors along \(V.\iota \circ f = (g : V \to U) \circ U.\iota\)),
187	  and \texttt{pullbackObjUnitToUnit} (pullback of unit is unit; uses
188	  \(\mathtt{F.Final}\) instance for the underlying continuous functor of an
189	  open immersion).
190	
191	  % NOTE (iter-188 plan-phase): Body lands with 1 narrow named typed sorry
192	  % on the residual chart-iso construction; the affine-chart existence step
193	  % is closed axiom-clean. Closure target iter-188 prover, ~30-50 LOC.
194	\end{proof}

196	\section{The pullback functor along the projection}
197	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_projection}
198	
199	If \(f : Y \to Z\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
200	\(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module, the pullback \(f^*\mathcal{M}\) is an invertible
201	\(\mathcal{O}_Y\)-module (the pullback of an open cover trivialising \(\mathcal{M}\)
202	trivialises \(f^*\mathcal{M}\), and the pullback of a free module of rank one is
203	free of rank one). This is a standard preservation fact (Stacks tag 01HH); we
204	specialise it to the projection \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and the
205	\(\mathcal{O}_T\)-Picard group.
206	
207	\begin{definition}\leanok
208	[Pullback along the projection]
209	  \label{def:pullback_along_projection}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
213	  % Definition df:aPf (functoriality of the absolute Picard functor)
214	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1290).
215	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
216	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
217	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
218	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
219	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
220	  %  [\dots]
221	  %  The absolute Picard functor  is a ``prepared presheaf'' in this
222	  % sense: given any family of  \(S\)-schemes \(T_i\), we have
223	  %     \(\)\Pic_X\bigl(\textstyle\coprod T_i\bigr)=\prod\Pic_X(T_i).\(\)"
224	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
225	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' and the ``prepared presheaf'' paragraph
226	  (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HH).}
227	  Let \(C/k\) and \(T/k\) be as in
228	  \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\)
229	  denote the canonical projection. The \emph{pullback along \(\pi_T\)} is the
230	  group homomorphism (in fact, the set map; the additive structure is not used
231	  in this chapter)
232	  \[
233	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T),
234	    \qquad [\mathcal{N}] \;\longmapsto\; [\pi_T^* \mathcal{N}],
235	  \]
236	  sending an isomorphism class of an invertible \(\mathcal{O}_T\)-module
237	  \(\mathcal{N}\) to the isomorphism class of \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N}\), an invertible
238	  \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)-module (Stacks tag 01HH). This assignment is well
239	  defined because pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules respects isomorphism, and is
240	  multiplicative on tensor products. It defines the functor
241	  \(\pi^* \mathrm{Pic} \subseteq \Pic_C\) that we quotient by in the definition of
242	  the relative Picard presheaf.
243	\end{definition}
244

252	\section{Composition of pullbacks}
253	\label{sec:lbp_compose}
254	
255	\begin{lemma}\leanok
256	[Composition of line-bundle pullbacks]
257	  \label{lem:pullback_compose}
258	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback_pullback_eq}
259	  
260	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
261	  % Definition df:aPf (the absolute Picard functor as a functor; functoriality
262	  % includes composition)
263	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1279).
264	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
265	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
266	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
267	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
268	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)"
269	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
270	  Def.~``absolute Picard functor'' --- the word \emph{functor} encodes both the
271	  identity and composition laws (cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG: pullback of an invertible
272	  module is invertible and compatible with composition).}
273	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
274	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. Write
275	  \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) and \(\pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T'\) for the
276	  canonical projections, and write
277	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\) for the
278	  base change of \(g\) along \(C/k\). Then on line bundles we have the equality of
279	  pullback maps
280	  \[
281	    g^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;=\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
282	    \quad\colon\quad \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T'),
283	  \]
284	  and, equivalently, the canonical natural isomorphism of pullback functors
285	  \[
286	    g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* \;\;\cong\;\; \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*
287	  \]
288	  realises both sides as the pullback along the composite morphism
289	  \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'} : C \times_k T' \to T\).
290	\end{lemma}
291	
292	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: not applicable --- the statement is one application of
293	% the functoriality of pullback (Kleiman § 2's "absolute Picard functor is a
294	% functor" + Stacks 01HG); no separate proof body is offered in the cited
295	% source, only the structural assertion. The Lean side is therefore a direct
296	% appeal to Mathlib's pullback-of-modules transitivity.
297	\begin{proof}
298	  
299	  
300	\leanok
301	  The square
302	  \[
303	    \begin{array}{ccc}
304	      C \times_k T' & \xrightarrow{g_C} & C \times_k T \\
305	      \;\downarrow\,\pi_{T'} & & \;\downarrow\,\pi_T \\
306	      T' & \xrightarrow{g} & T
307	    \end{array}
308	  \]
309	  is a pullback square (by definition of the fibre product). Equality of the
310	  composites \(\pi_T \circ g_C = g \circ \pi_{T'}\) holds on the nose. Pullback of
311	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules is functorial, so for any \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\)
312	  there is a canonical isomorphism
313	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \cong \pi_{T'}^* g^* \mathcal{N}\)
314	  obtained as the composite of pullback-along-composition isomorphisms
315	  \((\pi_T \circ g_C)^*\mathcal{N} \cong (g \circ \pi_{T'})^*\mathcal{N}\)
316	  (Mathlib \texttt{Module.pullback\_comp} on the underlying \(\mathcal{O}\)-module
317	  category, or Stacks tag 01HG for the category-theoretic statement). Passing
318	  to isomorphism classes turns this canonical isomorphism into the equality of
319	  set-valued maps stated above.
320	\end{proof}

322	\section{The relative Picard presheaf (set-valued)}
323	\label{sec:lbp_relpic_quotient}
324	
325	We now package the pullback functor into the relative Picard presheaf. As
326	explained in the planner note at the top of the chapter, we work with the
327	Set-valued version; the group refinement is deferred to A.1.c.
328	
329	\begin{theorem}\leanok
330	[The relative Picard quotient is well defined]
331	  \label{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
332	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage_subgroup}
333	  
334	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
335	  % Definition df:Pfs (relative Picard functor)
336	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
337	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
338	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
339	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
340	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
341	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
342	  % topologies  by
343	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
344	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
345	  %  \end{dfn}"
346	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
347	  Def.~``relative Picard functor'' df:Pfs.}
348	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product} and let \(T\) be a
349	  \(k\)-scheme. The subset
350	  \[
351	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T) \;\;\subseteq\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
352	  \]
353	  is the image of the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\)
354	  (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}); in particular it contains the trivial
355	  class and is closed under the multiplication of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) (so it is
356	  a subgroup). Define
357	  \[
358	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;:=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T,
359	  \]
360	  the quotient set whose elements are equivalence classes \([\mathcal{L}]\) of
361	  line bundles \(\mathcal{L} \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\) under the relation
362	  \(\mathcal{L} \sim \mathcal{L}'\) iff
363	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}'^{-1} \in H_T\).
364	  Then \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a well-defined assignment from
365	  \(k\)-schemes to sets, and the natural map
366	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjection of sets.
367	\end{theorem}
368

382	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate "proof" block for
383	% df:Pfs --- the definition's well-definedness is a direct group-theoretic
384	% observation. The verbatim statement above is the full source content. The

387	\begin{proof}
388	  
389	  
390	\leanok
391	  The subset \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\)
392	  because the pullback map \(\pi_T^*\) is a homomorphism of abelian groups (its
393	  multiplicativity on tensor products is part of
394	  \cref{def:pullback_along_projection}). Therefore the quotient
395	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is a well-defined quotient set (equivalently, a
396	  quotient abelian group; we forget the group structure to obtain the
397	  Set-valued version per the planner directive). The surjection
398	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is the canonical quotient map
399	  to equivalence classes; surjectivity is by construction.
400	\end{proof}
401	
402	\section{Naturality in the test scheme}
403	\label{sec:lbp_pullback_natural}
404	
405	\begin{theorem}\leanok
406	[Pullback descends through the quotient]
407	  \label{thm:pullback_natural}
408	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
409	  
410	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", §2 (FGA Explained Ch.9 §9.2),
411	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is a functor)
412	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
413	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
414	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
415	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
416	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
417	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
418	  %  [\dots]
419	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
420	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
421	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
422	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', §2,
423	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the word \emph{functor} encodes naturality in \(T\);
424	  cf.\ Stacks tag 01HG).}
425	  Let \(C/k\) be as in \cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}, and let
426	  \(g : T' \to T\) be a morphism of \(k\)-schemes. The pullback maps
427	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) and
428	  \(g^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(T')\) make the diagram
429	  \[
430	    \begin{array}{ccc}
431	      \Pic(T) & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_T^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T) \\
432	      \;\downarrow\,g^* & & \;\downarrow\,g_C^* \\
433	      \Pic(T') & \xrightarrow{\;\;\pi_{T'}^*\;\;} & \Pic(C \times_k T')
434	    \end{array}
435	  \]
436	  commute (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}). Therefore \(g_C^*\) sends
437	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\) and hence induces a
438	  unique set map
439	  \[
440	    g^\sharp \;\;\colon\;\; \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\longrightarrow\;
441	                              \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T'),
442	    \qquad [\mathcal{L}] \;\longmapsto\; [g_C^* \mathcal{L}],
443	  \]
444	  through the quotients of
445	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}. The assignment
446	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) satisfies \(\mathrm{id}_T^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
447	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) for any composable
448	  \(h : T'' \to T'\) and \(g : T' \to T\); thus
449	  \(T \mapsto \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) extends to a functor
450	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\).
451	\end{theorem}
452	
453	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: As with df:Pfs, Kleiman §2 does not supply a separate
454	% proof body for the functoriality assertion --- it is part of the definition
455	% (the word "functor" carries the identity and composition laws). The verbatim
456	% quote is therefore the same as the statement.
457	\begin{proof}
458	  
459	  
460	\leanok
461	  Commutativity of the displayed square is the special case
462	  \(g_C^* \circ \pi_T^* = \pi_{T'}^* \circ g^*\) of
463	  \cref{lem:pullback_compose}. Therefore \(g_C^*\) maps the image
464	  \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) into the image
465	  \(H_{T'} = \pi_{T'}^*\Pic(T')\): an element
466	  \(\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \in H_T\) with \(\mathcal{N} \in \Pic(T)\) has
467	  \(g_C^*\pi_T^*\mathcal{N} = \pi_{T'}^*g^*\mathcal{N} \in H_{T'}\). Hence
468	  \(g_C^*\) induces a unique map of quotient sets
469	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T \to \Pic(C \times_k T')/H_{T'}\) by the
470	  universal property of the quotient set
471	  (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}). The identity law
472	  \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) is immediate from
473	  \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) for pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules. The
474	  composition law \((g \circ h)^\sharp = g^\sharp \circ h^\sharp\) follows from
475	  the corresponding statement for \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback
476	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) (passing through the quotient is functorial).
477	  Hence \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\) is a presheaf.
478	\end{proof}
479	
480	\section{Lean encoding}
481	\label{sec:lbp_lean_encoding}
482	
483	The Lean target file is the new
484	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/LineBundlePullback.lean}, sibling to
485	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/RelativeSpec.lean}
486	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}). The blueprint declarations correspond to
487	Lean targets as follows.
488	
489	\begin{itemize}
490	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.OnProduct}
491	    (\cref{def:line_bundle_on_product}) is a re-export / type alias for the
492	    Mathlib invertible-module predicate on \(C \times_k T\). Mathlib provides
493	    \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Invertible} (or the equivalent
494	    bundle-of-rank-one packaging in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.LineBundle});
495	    the project-side declaration simply specialises the Mathlib predicate to
496	    the product scheme.
497	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullbackAlongProjection}
498	    (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}) is the pullback map on Picard
499	    groups induced by \(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\). The Mathlib backbone is
500	    the pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules
501	    (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Pullback}); the project-side
502	    declaration restricts that pullback to invertible modules and proves
503	    invertibility is preserved (Stacks tag 01HH; one open-cover unfolding).
504	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.LineBundle.pullback\_pullback\_eq}
505	    (\cref{lem:pullback_compose}) is the equality of pullback maps on
506	    isomorphism classes; the Lean form is the propositional equality of two
507	    \texttt{LinearMap}s (or \texttt{Quotient.mk}s) deduced from Mathlib's
508	    pullback-of-modules transitivity.
509	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.preimage\_subgroup}
510	    (\cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}) is the
511	    structural existence of the quotient set
512	    \(\Pic(C \times_k T)/H_T\). Mathlib's quotient-set machinery
513	    (\texttt{Quotient}, \texttt{QuotientGroup}) suffices; the project-side
514	    declaration only packages the universal property in the form
515	    \texttt{CategoryTheory.Functor.RepresentableBy} expects.
516	  \item \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.RelPicPresheaf.functorial}
517	    (\cref{thm:pullback_natural}) is the functor instance
518	    \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \mathrm{Set}\). The naturality square
519	    of \cref{lem:pullback_compose} is the data; the identity and composition
520	    laws are the lemmas \texttt{id\_eq} and \texttt{comp\_eq} of the
521	    quotient-induced map.
522	\end{itemize}
523	
524	No new core axiom is required. The chapter is a definitional / structural
525	construction on top of the existing Mathlib invertible-module + pullback
526	infrastructure, plus the project's relative-spectrum chapter
527	(\cref{chap:Picard_RelativeSpec}) for any concrete Lean unfolding that the
528	prover wishes to perform on an affine open of \(C \times_k T\).

25	% is abelian under tensor product, the image \(\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is a subgroup

35	\section{Setup and motivation}
36	\label{sec:relpic_setup}
37	
38	Fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper geometrically integral curve \(C\) over
39	\(k\). As in \cref{chap:Picard_LineBundlePullback}, write
40	\(\pi_T : C \times_k T \to T\) for the projection of a relative curve over a
41	\(k\)-scheme \(T\) and
42	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) for the induced pullback of
43	isomorphism classes of line bundles (\cref{def:pullback_along_projection}).
44	
45	The set-valued relative Picard presheaf
46	\[
47	  \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, \pi_T^*\Pic(T)
48	\]
49	of \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} was set up forgetting the
50	abelian-group structure inherited from tensor product. This chapter restores
51	that structure and then sheafifies. The first half (\S~REF
52	and \S~REF) verifies that the quotient is canonically
53	an abelian group and the functoriality of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} respects
54	that group structure; the result is a group-valued presheaf
55	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} : (\Sch/k)^{op} \to \Ab\) on the category of \(k\)-schemes. The
56	second half (\S~REF) takes the \'etale sheafification
57	\(\Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et} := (\Pic^\sharp_{C/k})^\sim\) in the \'etale topology on
58	\((\Sch/k)^{op}\), and verifies that sheafification preserves the abelian-group
59	target. The \'etale-sheafified functor is the object represented by the Picard
60	scheme in \cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}.
61	
62	\section{Group structure on the relative Picard quotient}
63	\label{sec:relpic_group}
64	
65	The Picard group \(\Pic(X)\) of any scheme is canonically an abelian group under
66	tensor product of line bundles (Stacks tag 01CR; the inverse of \([\mathcal{L}]\)
67	is $[\mathcal{L}^{-1}] = [\mathcal{H}om_{\mathcal{O}_X}(\mathcal{L},
68	\mathcal{O}_X)]$). The pullback map
69	\(\pi_T^* : \Pic(T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T)\) of
70	\cref{def:pullback_along_projection} respects this structure: pullback of
71	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules sends \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\)
72	and is multiplicative on tensor products.
73	
74	\begin{lemma}\leanok
75	[Group structure descends to the relative Picard quotient]
76	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}
77	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.addCommGroup}
78	  
79	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
80	  % Definition df:aPf + Definition df:Pfs (the absolute and relative Picard
81	  % functors are \emph{abelian-group}-valued, by construction; the relative
82	  % Picard functor is defined as a quotient \emph{group})
83	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
84	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
85	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
86	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
87	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
88	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
89	  %  [\dots]
90	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
91	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
92	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
93	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
94	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the Picard functors are abelian-group-valued, and the
95	  relative one is a quotient of abelian groups; cf.\ Stacks tag 01CR for the
96	  abelian-group structure on \(\Pic\)).}
97	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve and let \(T\) be a
98	  \(k\)-scheme. The Picard group \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) is an abelian group under
99	  tensor product of line bundles, and the pullback map
100	  \[
101	    \pi_T^* \;\colon\; \Pic(T) \;\longrightarrow\; \Pic(C \times_k T)
102	  \]
103	  of \cref{def:pullback_along_projection} is a group homomorphism. Its image
104	  \[
105	    H_T \;\;:=\;\; \pi_T^* \Pic(T)
106	  \]
107	  is therefore a subgroup of \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\), and the quotient set of
108	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined}
109	  \[
110	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \;\;=\;\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \, / \, H_T
111	  \]
112	  inherits a canonical abelian-group structure under which the quotient map
113	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \twoheadrightarrow \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a surjective
114	  homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
115	\end{lemma}
116	
117	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not supply a separate "proof" block
118	% for df:Pfs; the abelian-group structure is part of the definition (the word
119	% \emph{group} appears in df:aPf already, and df:Pfs is a quotient of the same
120	% abelian groups). The verbatim quotes for the statements above are therefore
121	% the same as for the lemma. The project-side proof is a structural restatement.
122	\begin{proof}
123	  
124	  The abelian-group structure on \(\Pic(C \times_k T)\) under tensor product is
125	  standard (Stacks tag 01CR or Hartshorne II~\S 6): for line bundles
126	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}'\), the tensor product
127	  \(\mathcal{L} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \mathcal{L}'\) is again
128	  invertible, the structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) is the unit, and
129	  the dual \(\mathcal{H}om(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T})\) is the
130	  inverse. Pullback of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules sends
131	  \(\mathcal{O}_T\) to \(\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}\) (the structure sheaf is
132	  preserved by inverse image) and satisfies
133	  $\pi_T^*(\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_T} \mathcal{N}') \cong
134	   \pi_T^*\mathcal{N} \otimes_{\mathcal{O}_{C \times_k T}} \pi_T^*\mathcal{N}'$
135	  by the standard tensor compatibility of pullback. Therefore \(\pi_T^*\)
136	  preserves the unit, sends inverses to inverses, and is multiplicative on
137	  tensor products; it is a homomorphism of abelian groups.
138	
139	  The image \(H_T = \pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) is then a subgroup. The quotient set
140	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) of
141	  \cref{thm:relative_pic_quotient_well_defined} carries the canonical quotient
142	  abelian-group structure (Lagrange's theorem on cosets specialised to an
143	  abelian quotient): for \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{L}' \in \Pic(C \times_k T)\), set
144	  \([\mathcal{L}] + [\mathcal{L}'] := [\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{L}']\);
145	  well-definedness follows from the subgroup condition. The canonical map
146	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T) / H_T\) is by construction a
147	  surjective group homomorphism with kernel exactly \(H_T\).
148	\end{proof}
149	
150	\section{The relative Picard presheaf as a group-valued presheaf}
151	\label{sec:relpic_groupvalued}
152	
153	We now refine the set-valued functor of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} into a
154	group-valued presheaf.
155	
156	\begin{definition}\leanok
157	[Relative Picard presheaf]
158	  \label{def:rel_pic_sharp}
159	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp}
160	  
161	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
162	  % Definition df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor)
163	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1311-L1318).
164	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
165	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
166	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
167	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)
168	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
169	  % topologies  by
170	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\
171	  %     \Pic_{(X/S)\fppf}.\(\)
172	  %  \end{dfn}"
173	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
174	  Def.~df:Pfs.}
175	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
176	  \emph{relative Picard presheaf} of \(C\) over \(k\) is the functor
177	  \[
178	    \Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \;\;\colon\;\; (\Sch/k)^{op} \;\longrightarrow\; \Ab,
179	    \qquad
180	    T \;\longmapsto\; \Pic(C \times_k T) \,/\, \pi_T^*\Pic(T),
181	  \]
182	  where the right-hand side is the quotient abelian group of
183	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the structure morphisms are induced by
184	  the pullback maps \(g^\sharp\) of \cref{thm:pullback_natural} (which restrict
185	  to group homomorphisms on the quotient because the source square commutes
186	  with all pullback functors, see the proof below). In Kleiman's notation with
187	  \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\), this is exactly \(\Pic_{X/S}\) of df:Pfs.
188	\end{definition}
189	
190	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
191	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp at the pinned Mathlib commit
192	% b80f227 carries a constant-functor placeholder body
193	% ((CategoryTheory.Functor.const _).obj (AddCommGrpCat.of PUnit)),
194	% i.e.\ the constant functor at the trivial abelian group. This is NOT
195	% the relative Picard presheaf
196	% \(T \mapsto \Pic(C \times_k T)/\pi_T^*\Pic(T)\) that the definition
197	% above describes; the placeholder merely supplies a type-checking
198	% object of the correct kind. The closure is gated on the upstream

207	\begin{lemma}\leanok
208	[Naturality respects the group structure]
209	  \label{lem:rel_pic_sharp_functorial}
210	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial}
211	  
212	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], "The Picard scheme", \S 2 (FGA Explained Ch.~9 \S 9.2),
213	  % Definitions df:aPf + df:Pfs (the relative Picard functor is functorial
214	  % into abelian groups; the word \emph{functor} into the category of abelian
215	  % groups encodes both the identity / composition laws and the
216	  % group-homomorphism property of the restriction maps)
217	  % (read from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex, L1274-L1318).
218	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
219	  % "The {\it absolute Picard functor} \(\Pic_X\) is the functor from  the
220	  %  category of (locally Noetherian) \(S\)-schemes \(T\) to the category of
221	  % abelian groups defined by the formula
222	  %     \(\)\Pic_X(T):=\Pic(X_T).\(\)
223	  %  [\dots]
224	  %  \begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}
225	  %   The {\it relative Picard functor} \(\Pic_{X/S}\) is defined by
226	  %     \(\)\Pic_{X/S}(T):=\Pic(X_T)\big/\Pic(T).\(\)"
227	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
228	  Defs.~df:aPf + df:Pfs (the target category is the category of abelian
229	  groups; the structure maps are group homomorphisms).}
230	  For a morphism \(g : T' \to T\) of \(k\)-schemes, the induced set map
231	  \(g^\sharp : \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T')\) of
232	  \cref{thm:pullback_natural} is a homomorphism of abelian groups with respect
233	  to the structure of \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, and the identity and
234	  composition laws \(\mathrm{id}^\sharp = \mathrm{id}\) and
235	  \((g \circ h)^\sharp = h^\sharp \circ g^\sharp\) hold as identities of group
236	  homomorphisms.
237	\end{lemma}
238	
239	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: Kleiman \S 2 does not write out a separate proof for
240	% functoriality; the structural assertion is part of the definition. No
241	% verbatim proof body is available in the source; the project-side argument
242	% is a direct unfolding of the pullback of modules.
243	% NOTE (iter-199 plan agent): the Lean closure
244	% AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.functorial at b80f227 carries a
245	% zero AddMonoidHom body (\(0 : \dots \to \dots\)), i.e.\ the zero map
246	% between two trivial-group endpoints, NOT the pullback-descended
247	% homomorphism this lemma describes. Moreover, because the codomain's
248	% \texttt{Zero} instance is derived from the sorry-body
249	% \texttt{addCommGroup} (the file-local genuine sorry of the chapter),
250	% the closure also inherits \texttt{sorryAx} via that typeclass leak;
251	% it is therefore not even axiom-clean. The closure is gated on the
252	% upstream Mathlib \texttt{Scheme.Modules} monoidal-structure gap. DO
253	% NOT promote this block to \leanok via the deterministic
254	% \texttt{sync\_leanok} phase, and DO NOT mark it formalised in
255	% review, until the body is replaced with the mathematically correct
256	% map descended from \(g_C^*\).
257	\begin{proof}
258	
259	  By \cref{thm:pullback_natural}, the set map \(g^\sharp\) is defined by
260	  \([\mathcal{L}] \mapsto [g_C^* \mathcal{L}]\), where
261	  \(g_C := \mathrm{id}_C \times_k g : C \times_k T' \to C \times_k T\). Pullback
262	  of \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules along \(g_C\) preserves tensor products and the
263	  structure sheaf, hence the map
264	  \(g_C^* : \Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic(C \times_k T')\) on Picard groups is
265	  itself an abelian-group homomorphism. By
266	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}, the quotient map
267	  \(\Pic(C \times_k T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is a group homomorphism, and
268	  \(g_C^*\) takes \(H_T\) into \(H_{T'}\) by \cref{lem:pullback_compose}; therefore
269	  \(g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism on the quotient. The identity and
270	  composition laws are inherited from \(\mathrm{id}^* = \mathrm{id}\) and
271	  \((g \circ h)^* = h^* \circ g^*\) on \(\mathcal{O}\)-module pullback.
272	\end{proof}

316	
317	  The object map is well defined by
318	  \cref{lem:rel_pic_sharp_groupoid}: for each \(k\)-scheme \(T\), the value
319	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T)\) is an abelian group. The morphism map
320	  \(g \mapsto g^\sharp\) is a group homomorphism by

327	\section{\'Etale sheafification}
328	\label{sec:relpic_etale_sheaf}
329	
330	The presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) is generally not an \'etale (or even Zariski)
331	sheaf. The standard obstruction (Kleiman \S 2, L1292--L1302): for a \(k\)-scheme
332	\(T\) carrying a non-trivial line bundle whose pullback to \(C \times_k T\) becomes
333	non-trivial after an open cover, the Zariski separated-presheaf condition
334	fails. Therefore, to obtain a representable functor in the sense of
335	\cref{chap:Picard_FGAPicRepresentability}, one must replace
336	\(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) by its \emph{associated \'etale sheaf}. Kleiman \S 2 names
337	this sheaf \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) and singles it out as the functor that Kleiman's
338	main existence theorem \S 4 (th:main) represents.
339	
340	\begin{definition}\leanok
341	[\'Etale sheafification of the relative Picard presheaf]
342	  \label{def:rel_pic_etale_sheafification}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf}
344	  % NOTE (iter-176 review): the iter-176 Lane G prover renamed this declaration
345	  % from ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme`` to
346	  % ``AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicSharp.etSheaf`` to avoid a name collision with
347	  % ``Picard_FGAPicRepresentability`` (which legitimately uses ``PicScheme``
348	  % for the REPRESENTING SCHEME, a different mathematical object). The
349	  % ``\lean{...}`` pin is updated accordingly. The §"Lean encoding" bullet 5
350	  % below is also corrected.

358	  % "\begin{dfn}\label{df:Pfs}

361	  %  Denote its associated sheaves in the Zariski, \'etale, and fppf
362	  % topologies  by
363	  %     \(\)\Pic_{(X/S)\zar},\ \Pic_{(X/S)\et},\

365	  %  \end{dfn}

367	  %  if the latter functor is one of the three just displayed,
368	  %  then it is a sheaf in the indicated topology; in fact, it is the sheaf
369	  %  associated to any one of its predecessors, and the map between them is

374	  \textit{Source: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 2,
375	  Def.~df:Pfs (the \'etale-sheaf notation \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\)) and the
376	  associated-sheaf paragraph immediately following df:Pfs.}
377	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve. The
378	  \emph{\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor} is the sheafification of
379	  the presheaf \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) (\cref{thm:rel_pic_sharp_presheaf}) in the
380	  global \'etale topology on \((\Sch/k)\):

386	  isomorphism) sheaf of abelian groups on the \'etale site of \(\Sch/k\)

388	  \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}$ which is universal among presheaf morphisms from
389	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}\) to \'etale sheaves of abelian groups. In Kleiman's
390	  notation this is \(\Pic_{(X/S)\et}\) with \(X = C\) and \(S = \Spec k\). When \(k\)
391	  is algebraically closed and \(T = \Spec k'\) for an algebraically closed field
392	  extension \(k'/k\), the natural map
393	  \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k}(T) \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}(T)\) is a bijection (Kleiman
394	  Exercise ex:Alr, L1357--L1361).
395	\end{definition}

499	
500	  The canonical morphism from a presheaf to its associated sheaf in
501	  any Grothendieck topology (Stacks tag 00WI; the canonical
502	  sheafification unit) supplies, in particular, a morphism of
503	  presheaves \(\Pic^\sharp_{C/k} \to \Pic^\sharp_{(C/k)\et}\); the
